words.shortDefinition: Strategy using a large ERP for headquarters and smaller ones for subsidiaries.
words.longDefinition: Two-tier ERP is a technology strategy where an organization uses a robust, highly-customized 'Tier 1' ERP at the corporate headquarters, and a more agile, cost-effective 'Tier 2' ERP for its smaller subsidiaries or regional offices.
words.techContext: Our company uses SAP at the global level, but we implemented NetSuite as a Tier 2 ERP for our new startup acquisitions to maintain speed and flexibility.
words.exampleSentence: The two-tier ERP approach allowed the local branch to go live in three months while still syncing its financial data to the corporate hub.

words.shortDefinition: Database that stores data in RAM instead of on disk for extreme speed.
words.longDefinition: An in-memory database (IMDB) relies primarily on main memory for computer data storage, allowing for significantly faster data access and real-time processing of massive datasets.
words.techContext: SAP HANA is the most well-known in-memory database for ERPs, enabling complex financial reports that used to take hours to run in seconds.
words.exampleSentence: Migrating to an in-memory database allowed us to perform real-time inventory adjustments across all our global warehouses.
words.etymology: Descriptive phrase for data stored in 'memory' (RAM).

words.shortDefinition: Architecture style where components provide services to other components via a protocol.
words.longDefinition: Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) is a style of software design where services are provided to the other components by application components, through a communication protocol over a network.
words.techContext: Many legacy on-premise ERPs were rebuilt using SOA to allow third-party applications to interact with specific business functions through web services.
words.exampleSentence: The ERP's SOA structure made it much easier to integrate our custom-built mobile app with the core order-entry service.

words.shortDefinition: Deconstructed ERP strategy using loosely coupled cloud and on-premise apps.
words.longDefinition: Postmodern ERP is a strategy where an organization moves away from a single, monolithic ERP suite in favor of a mix of loosely coupled cloud-based and on-premise applications that are integrated based on business needs.
words.techContext: Gartner defines postmodern ERP as the shift from a 'one size fits all' suite to a federated ecosystem of specialized business applications.
words.exampleSentence: Our shift to a postmodern ERP strategy allowed us to keep our legacy finance core while using a best-in-class cloud app for talent management.
words.etymology: From 'postmodern' (representing a departure from modernism) and 'ERP'.

words.shortDefinition: Switching to a new ERP system for the whole company at once.
words.longDefinition: A Big Bang implementation is an ERP rollout strategy where the entire organization switches from the old system to the new one on a single date.
words.techContext: Big Bang implementations are high-risk but high-reward, as they eliminate the need to maintain expensive integrations between the old and new systems.
words.exampleSentence: Despite the risks, the CEO chose a Big Bang implementation to ensure the entire company was on the same platform before the start of the fiscal year.
words.etymology: Metaphor from cosmology, referring to a single, explosive start.

words.shortDefinition: Rolling out an ERP in stages, either by module or by location.
words.longDefinition: A phased implementation is a rollout strategy where the ERP is introduced in a series of steps, such as deploying one module at a time or implementing the system at one subsidiary at a time.
words.techContext: We chose a phased implementation by geography, starting with our smallest office in Singapore to test the system configuration before tackling the US headquarters.
words.exampleSentence: The phased implementation took longer than a Big Bang approach, but it significantly reduced the operational risk to the business.

words.shortDefinition: Running the old and new ERP systems simultaneously for a period.
words.longDefinition: Parallel implementation is a rollout strategy where both the legacy system and the new ERP system are operated at the same time to verify that the new system's outputs match the old one's.
words.techContext: We performed a parallel implementation for one month to ensure the payroll calculations in the new ERP were 100% accurate compared to the old system.
words.exampleSentence: Parallel implementation provides the highest level of security, but it requires double the data entry work for the staff.

words.shortDefinition: Structured approach to transitioning individuals and teams to a new system.
words.longDefinition: Change Management is the collective term for all approaches to prepare, support, and help individuals, teams, and organizations in making organizational change, particularly during a new ERP rollout.
words.techContext: ERP failures are rarely technical; they usually stem from poor change management and users resisting the new ways of working.
words.exampleSentence: We hired a dedicated change management lead to coordinate training sessions and internal communications for the ERP project.

words.shortDefinition: A committee of high-level executives that oversees an ERP project.
words.longDefinition: A Steering Committee (SteerCo) is a group of senior stakeholders who provide strategic direction, approve budgets, and resolve high-level conflicts during an ERP implementation.
words.techContext: The SteerCo met every month to review the project's progress against the budget and decide on major scope changes.
words.exampleSentence: We need to prepare a presentation for the SteerCo to justify the additional three weeks needed for the UAT phase.

words.shortDefinition: Testing performed by end-users to ensure the system meets business needs.
words.longDefinition: User Acceptance Testing (UAT) is the final phase of ERP testing where actual business users test the system in real-world scenarios to confirm it works as intended before go-live.
words.techContext: During UAT, the sales team discovered that the new order screen was missing a field for special delivery instructions.
words.exampleSentence: The go-live was postponed because several critical bugs were found during the final week of UAT.

words.shortDefinition: Testing that ensures all interconnected systems work together.
words.longDefinition: System Integration Testing (SIT) is a phase where the technical team verifies that the ERP can communicate correctly with all bolt-ons, APIs, and external databases.
words.techContext: The SIT phase revealed that the ERP was sending the wrong currency code to the third-party payment gateway.
words.exampleSentence: We cannot start UAT until the technical team completes SIT and confirms the data flows between CRM and ERP are stable.

words.shortDefinition: The practice of structuring data so every data element is mastered in one place.
words.longDefinition: Single Source of Truth (SSOT) is an information architecture concept where all data across an organization is stored in a way that anyone needing that data must access it from one primary location.
words.techContext: The primary goal of our ERP project is to establish a single source of truth for our inventory levels across all retail and online channels.
words.exampleSentence: Before the ERP, we had three different customer lists; now, the ERP serves as our single source of truth.
words.etymology: Descriptive phrase emphasizing a 'single' location for 'truthful' (accurate) data.

codeExample.code:
const ssot = erp.getMasterData('Product');
const cached = localStore.get('Product'); // Secondary source

words.shortDefinition: ERP system with no built-in UI, accessed entirely through APIs.
words.longDefinition: A headless ERP provides back-end business logic and data storage but does not have its own user interface, allowing developers to build custom front-ends for different devices.
words.techContext: By using a headless ERP, we were able to build a highly specialized React-based warehouse portal that is much faster than the standard vendor UI.
words.exampleSentence: The headless ERP approach allowed us to integrate our back-office functions seamlessly into our existing customer-facing mobile app.
words.etymology: From 'headless' (computing term for software without a GUI) and 'ERP'.

words.shortDefinition: Using ERP data to predict when equipment will fail before it happens.
words.longDefinition: Predictive Maintenance uses data from IoT sensors integrated with the ERP to monitor the condition of equipment and perform maintenance only when necessary, based on actual wear.
words.techContext: Our ERP's SCM module uses predictive maintenance to alert us two weeks before a conveyor belt is expected to snap based on its vibration patterns.
words.exampleSentence: Switching from a fixed schedule to predictive maintenance reduced our factory downtime by 30%.

words.shortDefinition: Software 'bots' that automate repetitive human tasks in an ERP.
words.longDefinition: Robotic Process Automation (RPA) uses software scripts to emulate human interaction with the ERP's user interface, performing repetitive tasks like data entry or invoice matching.
words.techContext: We implemented an RPA bot to take data from our legacy PDF invoices and enter it into the ERP's Accounts Payable module.
words.exampleSentence: RPA allowed us to handle a 50% increase in order volume without hiring more administrative staff.

words.shortDefinition: ERP built from interchangeable building blocks of business capabilities.
words.longDefinition: Composable ERP is an adaptive architecture where business capabilities are broken down into 'Packaged Business Capabilities' (PBCs) that can be easily assembled and reassembled.
words.techContext: In a composable ERP environment, if we find a better inventory algorithm, we can swap out that specific component without touching the rest of the system.
words.exampleSentence: The composable ERP model gives us the agility to respond to market changes by adding new business capabilities in weeks instead of months.

words.shortDefinition: ERP designed as a set of APIs before a user interface was built.
words.longDefinition: An API-First ERP prioritizes the development of robust, documented APIs as the primary way of interacting with the system, ensuring that all functionality is accessible to other applications.
words.techContext: Because our ERP is API-first, we never run into 'hidden' data that can only be seen in the vendor's UI but not extracted for reporting.
words.exampleSentence: The API-first nature of our new ERP allowed us to build a custom Alexa skill for warehouse managers to check stock levels by voice.

words.shortDefinition: Top-level management group that guides an ERP implementation.
words.longDefinition: A steering committee is a group of high-level stakeholders who provide guidance on a project's strategic direction and have the authority to approve major changes and funding.
words.techContext: The steering committee's primary role is to ensure the ERP project remains aligned with the company's long-term business goals.
words.exampleSentence: The steering committee decided to increase the project budget to include the new AI-driven forecasting module.

words.shortDefinition: Audit conducted after go-live to evaluate project success and lessons learned.
words.longDefinition: A Post-implementation Review (PIR) is a formal evaluation of the ERP project conducted 3-6 months after go-live to measure whether the business benefits were achieved.
words.techContext: The post-implementation review highlighted that while the system was stable, users needed more training on the reporting module.
words.exampleSentence: The results of the PIR helped us identify several process bottlenecks that we missed during the initial design phase.

words.shortDefinition: Process of fixing or removing incorrect data before an ERP migration.
words.longDefinition: Data cleansing (or scrubbing) is the process of identifying and correcting corrupt, inaccurate, or incomplete records from a dataset prior to importing them into a new ERP.
words.techContext: We spent six months on data cleansing, removing duplicate customer profiles and fixing inconsistent addresses.
words.exampleSentence: Without rigorous data cleansing, the new ERP will just produce 'garbage in, garbage out' results.

words.shortDefinition: An old computer system or application that is being replaced by an ERP.
words.longDefinition: A legacy system is an outdated software application or technology that is still in use but is scheduled for replacement because it no longer meets business needs or is too expensive to maintain.
words.techContext: The biggest challenge of the project was extracting data from our 20-year-old legacy system that had no API and used a proprietary database.
words.exampleSentence: We are keeping the legacy system in read-only mode for two years for historical auditing purposes.

words.shortDefinition: Moving an on-premise ERP system to the cloud.
words.longDefinition: Cloud migration is the process of moving data, applications, and IT processes from an organization's on-premise servers to a cloud-based infrastructure.
words.techContext: The CFO advocated for cloud migration to shift our ERP costs from a capital expense (servers) to an operating expense (subscriptions).
words.exampleSentence: Our cloud migration strategy involves a 'lift and shift' of the database followed by a gradual refactoring of our custom code.

words.shortDefinition: Software that sits between an ERP and other applications to translate data.
words.longDefinition: In an ERP environment, middleware acts as a translation layer that allows the ERP to exchange data with external systems like e-commerce platforms or third-party logistics (3PL) providers.
words.techContext: We used MuleSoft as our middleware to connect our ERP's SOAP web services to our warehouse's modern REST API.
words.exampleSentence: The middleware handles the mapping between our internal product codes and the different formats required by our global vendors.

words.shortDefinition: Cloud ERP where a customer has their own dedicated instance and database.
words.longDefinition: Single-tenant cloud is a software architecture where a single instance of the software and supporting infrastructure serves just one customer, providing higher security and more customization options than multi-tenant cloud.
words.techContext: The bank insisted on a single-tenant cloud ERP to ensure their data was physically isolated from other customers' data.
words.exampleSentence: While more expensive, the single-tenant cloud model allowed us to control exactly when our ERP system was upgraded.

words.shortDefinition: Evaluation of how well an ERP system matches business requirements.
words.longDefinition: A Fit-Gap analysis is a phase in the ERP selection or design process where business requirements are compared against the software's features to identify 'fits' (standard features) and 'gaps' (missing features that need customization).
words.techContext: Our fit-gap analysis showed that the standard ERP could handle 90% of our finance needs but had a major gap in our complex subscription billing logic.
words.exampleSentence: The results of the fit-gap analysis will determine whether we need to hire more developers for custom coding or change our business processes.
